content:
Eliminate, Defer, Parallelize, Colocate

5 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

When dealing with slow frontend applications, identifying a bottleneck is only half the battle. Once you know a page is slow, how do you fix it? It feels like most frontend performance advice is a set of buzzwords (code-splitting, memoization, CDNs), but it isn’t clear how to choose the right solution for the problem at hand.
After attending a recent tech talk at HubSpot, I have realized that many performance improvement strategies share similar themes. We can group these strategies by theme to develop a universal framework for dealing with frontend performance issues. Outlined below are four Pillars that contain tools to address common themes of these performance bottlenecks.
Pillar 1: Eliminate
Elimination is essentially all about doing less work. How do you optimize a process? First, ask if it needs to exist at all.
Network-Level Elimination
If your bundle is pulling in way too much unused code, you could be killing your Largest Contentful Paint (LCP). Tree-Shaking alleviates this pain by splitting up unused JavaScript from the final bundle. Most modern bundlers have this built in, but there are a few anti-patterns that could be making your bundle larger than it needs to be. For example:
// Prevents Tree-Shaking
import _ from 'lodash';// Tree-Shakable (Only pulls in the debounce function)
import { debounce } from 'lodash-es';
Execution-Level Elimination
Another way we can easily eliminate work is to make sure we aren’t performing expensive network requests or computations more than necessary. With caching we can store the results of heavy API calls so we don’t have to wait for a network response every time we need the same data. The trade-off here is that we need to be careful about our fetch policies (i.e. cache-first, network-only) so we don’t incorrectly use stale data or unnecessarily skip our cache.
Similarly, with memoization we can store the results of expensive CPU calculations, like parsing through and formatting a large list of data. The trade-off here is that memoization isn’t free. In frameworks like React, it can sometimes cost more to check if a dependency has changed than it costs to re-run a trivial function.
We can also use batching to reduce work. If you have a UI that is trying to make state updates every 2 milliseconds, you could be using requestAnimationFrame to batch these updates every frame for a smoother experience. We can also batch network requests so we don’t make unnecessary searches while a user is still inputting their query, for example.
DOM-Level Elimination
A final strategy to eliminate work is to only do work for what the user can actually see. If an expensive component lives way off the screen, why even bother rendering it? Virtualization is an example of this, where long lists can be optimized to only render what is near the screen (check out my in-depth guide to list virtualization here). Again, the trade-off here is that it does take work to decide what to render and where to position items, so applying virtualization to a small list of items could actually hurt overall performance.
Pillar 2: Defer
If you can’t eliminate your work, you may still be able to wait to do it. This is basically procrastination: wait to do work until you need to do it. More concretely, this is protecting the main thread from work that isn’t immediately needed.
Some useful tools for improving initial page load and LCP are Code-Splitting & Lazy Initialization. We can use dynamic import statements to delay loading components that the user won’t even see on an initial page load (like a heavy modal). Then, we can procrastinate until user interaction (like hovering the modal trigger) to import the bundle for the code-split module. Lazy initialization can also be used for computing expensive values, not just importing code.
We also want to defer work while users are performing actions. Imagine you are loading a large list of data, and the loading stutters because the site wants to show you a CSAT (Customer Satisfaction) popup survey (I know I wouldn’t give a good score!). The right tool here would be to use requestIdleCallback to wait for the browser’s main thread to be ready. This also helps protect INP (Interaction to Next Paint) by preventing long-running JavaScript from blocking user interactions.
Pillar 3: Parallelize
Rather than doing less work or deferring it until later, what if we just did more work simultaneously? Computers do this natively by distributing tasks across multiple CPU cores.
You might be thinking, “but isn’t JavaScript famously single-threaded?”, and you are absolutely right. The JavaScript engine itself can only execute one line of code at a time on the main thread. To get around this, JavaScript exposes asynchronous tools like callbacks, Promises, andasync/await so we don’t block the main thread, which directly protects our INP.
However, these tools don’t achieve true multi-threading on their own. Wrapping heavy function calls in async functions will still freeze the UI. The main purpose of these tools is to offload I/O tasks, like network requests, and let the event loop pick them back up from the task queue later.
In some environments, we can achieve true parallelism. For instance, most (if not all) browsers support Web Workers. When we create a Web Worker, the browser will allocate a completely new OS thread and new instance of JavaScript just for that worker. The one thing to note here is that these threads are isolated, and they must pass messages through an event channel to communicate (i.e. postMessage). If you pass a massive array of objects to a worker, the time spent serializing that data could block the main thread longer than just doing the work on the main thread.
It’s also worth briefly mentioning here that JavaScript runtime environments outside of the browser, like Node.js, often have built-in modules for spawning multiple threads. Node.js has worker_threads, for example.
Pillar 4: Colocate
Lastly, we can improve the efficiency of our application by storing resources closer to where they are consumed. This category is a bit broader and we can see it at many scales.
At a small scale, we can colocate state closer to where it’s being used. When we move state to the lowest common parent in a render tree, we prevent larger, unnecessary re-renders when only one child needs to update.
At a slightly larger scale, we can store some data directly on client machines. Instead of fetching and waiting for user preferences from the backend, we can store these infrequently changing values in localStorage.
At a much larger scale, we can even use CDNs (content delivery networks) to colocate assets geographically closer to users. When we have users on the other side of the world, caching and serving data from a location thousands of miles closer to the user significantly reduces their page load times.
Conclusion
With most frontend performance bottlenecks, we can pick a solution from one of four pillars.

• Can we delete, compress, or virtualize it? (Eliminate)

• Does it have to run right now? (Defer)

• Can we run it off the main thread? (Parallelize)

• Can we move this resource closer to the consumer? (Colocate)
